EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated high wind occurred ahead of a strong cold front which pushed through the Mojave Desert.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This gust occurred at Bicycle Lake (KBYS).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep, cold winter storm hit northern California after nearly 2 months of relatively dry weather pattern. This system began in the early morning hours of Feb. 19th and continued through the evening. This brought a significant amount of snowfall to the Sierra foothills and up to 10-12 of snow in the higher Sierra. A multi-car pile-up occurred due to the snow amounts. Furthermore, this system produced two landspout tornadoes in the Central Valley, and several funnel clouds, gustnados, and small hail were reported. Rainfall amounts ranged between 0.5 to 0.50 for the lower elevations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A cold low pressure system moved through Feb. 18th and 19th. A strong precipitation band with low snow levels (1000-2000 ft) dropped a significant amount of snow in the Sierra foothills. Snowfall totals were reported as follows: Camino (3120 ft) received 7, Fiddletown (2500 ft) received 4, Soulsbyville (3000 ft) received 7, Nevada City (3000-3300 ft) received 7 to 8.5, San Andreas (1720 ft) received 4, Grass Valley (2422 ft) received 3, Placerville (2180 ft) received 6, Sonora (3000 ft) received 7, and Pine Grove (2100 ft) received 2. During the event, a 30-car pile up occurred on Highway 4 (Ebbetts Pass Highway) and Forest Meadows Drive. No known fatalities.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong, upper-level trough brought colder weather along with rain, mountain snow and gusty southwest to west winds to the region. Light rain began during the early morning on the 19th, with most precipitation ending west of the mountains later that night. Showers lingered over the mountains and deserts into the 20th, as moisture wrapped around the surface low pressure system, exiting to the southeast. Coastal and inland valley locations generally received between one-tenth and one-half of an inch of rain, with up to one inch in the coastal foothills of San Diego and San Bernardino Counties. The upper deserts varied widely from no rain in Victorville to one-half inch at the Mojave Dam. Only a few hundredths were reported in the Coachella Valley. Snow levels lowered to 3500 to 4000 feet, bringing 2 to 5 inches of snow to mountain locations above 4500 feet and trace amounts below. Chains were required on Palomar Mountain. This was the last significant precipitation until late January of 2014. Cold air instability also resulted in scattered thunderstorms during the afternoon of the 19th in the valleys and high desert. Very small hail and flooding was reported by a spotter in Anaheim on Miraloma Street between Fee Ana Street and Lakeview, where water flooded over the curbs and in the intersection up to 6 inches deep in places. A spotter in Phelan sent photos of small hail covering their yard. The Coachella Valley saw the highest wind gusts overnight and into the morning of the 19th, with a few in the San Diego County mountains as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds occurred at the Volcan Mountain SDGE mesonet between 0150-0650 PST on the 19th with a peak gust of 66 mph recorded between 0420-0430 PST.|High winds occurred at the Harrison Park mesonet between 0200-0230 PST on the 19th with a peak gust of 58 mph recorded during that period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very cold and dry air mass brought the coldest temperatures in six years across the Mojave Desert.  Hard freezes occurred on four to five consecutive mornings in most places.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several pipes burst in Yucca Valley and Joshua Tree due to the cold, including some which caused water damage to buildings. A spotter in Yucca Valley measured a low temperature of 14 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold trough of low pressure dropped down the West Coast, before closing off over southern California. This system brought light to locally moderate showers to most areas, except the Coachella Valley, on the afternoon and evening of the 6th. Light showers lingered into the morning of the 7th near the mountains. Rainfall totals for the coast and valleys were mostly light, generally ranging from one-tenth to one-third of an inch, with three-quarters of an inch to an inch for the lower mountains and foothills. The lower deserts generally received less than one-tenth of an inch, with the exception of Borrego Springs with 0.16 inches of rain. The snow level fell to between 3500 and 4000 feet, with 1 to 3 inches of snow below 6000 feet, and up to 6 inches above 6000 feet.On the 10th, a very cold trough of low pressure from the northwest moved through southern California, bringing significant cooling, low snow levels, gusty west winds and light to locally moderate precipitation along and west of the mountains. The snow level dropped to around 2500 feet, with accumulating snow of around one-half inch down to around 3000 feet in locations such as Hesperia and the Cajon Pass in San Bernardino County, and Julian in San Diego County. Up to 5 inches of snow fell above 4000 feet. Chains were required in most mountain areas as well. Gusty winds also accompanied the storm on the 10th, even resulting in some blowing dust in the Coachella Valley. Strong winds across the coastal waters, combined with a large swell and astronomical high tides, resulted in minor coastal flooding during the morning of the 11th in La Jolla, Imperial Beach and Newport Beach. Most flooding was limited to a few beach parking lots and some nearby surface streets.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Sky Valley mesonet station recorded a peak gust of 76 mph around 1542 PST. During this afternoon, the  Thermal Airport ASOS measured a peak gust of 49 mph between 1251 and 1351 PST, with one-quarter mile visibility due to blowing dust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A coastal marine fog (warm air over cold water) resulted in areas of dense fog in the coastal San Diego area during the early morning of the 26th. The ceilings and low visibility in fog resulted in airport delays at Lindbergh Field.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dense fog was intermittent at Montgomery Field between 0500 and 1600 PST on the 26th with visibilities of one-quarter mile or less. The lowest visibility was one-eighth of a mile around 1445 PST. Visibility at Lindbergh Field was one-quarter mile at 0725 PST that morning. The low ceilings and visibility resulted in numerous flight delays.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold, upper-level trough of low pressure brought gusty west winds, and scattered showers to the mountains and San Diego County foothills on the 4th. Most areas that saw rain measured a quarter of an inch or less, but some up-slope foothill locations in San Diego County measured up to one-half of an inch. The snow level dropped to around 3000 feet, with a trace to two inches of snow measured from I-8 to Big Bear. Pine Valley schools were closed on the 4th and chain control was enforced around the Big Bear and Pine Cove areas. The strongest winds were fairly well distributed over the area with several gusts above 60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds began at the Volcan Mountain SDGE mesonet between 2210-2220 PST on the 3rd and ended between 0520 and 0530 PST on the 4th. During this time, sustained winds were around 40-50 mph with gusts mainly over 60 mph. A peak gust of 69 mph was measured twice between 0400-0420 PST on the 4th. |High winds began at the Harrison Park SDGE mesonet between 2240-2250 PST on the 3rd and ended between 0350 and 0400 PST on the 4th. During this time, sustained winds were around 39-44 mph with off and on gusts over 58 mph. A peak gust of 62 mph was measured twice between 0100-0400 PST on the 4th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 1042 mb high built in over Nevada on the 9th as the low moved off to the east with a 20-25 mb offshore pressure gradient setting up from Salt Lake City to San Diego. This produces gusty northeast winds and cool, dry air for southern California through the 10th. The San Diego County mountains and foothills were again the most windy areas for this event with gusts over 65 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds began at the Sill Hill SDGE mesonet between 0710-0720 PST on the 9th and ended between 1330-1340 PST that afternoon, with a brief resurgence between 1950-2040 PST that evening. During the main high wind period, winds were sustained 40-55 mph with gusts 60-70 mph. The peak gust of 75 mph was measured twice between 0850-0910 PST.|High winds occurred at the Buckman Springs SDGE mesonet between 0810-0820 PST with a peak gust of 59 mph measured during that period.|High winds occurred at the Inaja Park SDGE mesonet between 1120-1140 PST with a peak gust of 58 mph measured during that period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Surface high pressure peaked at 1045 mb over western Utah(similar in strength to December 9-10 event, which was centered in eastern Nevada) and appeared to be diurnally enhanced due to strong radiational cooling in the Utah desert. This setup resulted in moderate to locally strong Santa Ana winds. The winds developed in the mountains and spread into the inland valleys and coastal areas of Orange county on the 14th, with strengthening winds in San Diego County late in the day. A secondary peak of offshore winds occurred the night of the 14th through midday on the 15th with the strongest winds across the Riverside and San Diego County mountains and foothills. The highest wind gusts were observed in the mountains and foothills of San Diego County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds began at the Pleasants Peak mesonet between 0230-0300 PST and ended between 1100-1130 PST on the 14th. During this time, winds were sustained 40-50 mph out of the east-northeast, gusting mostly above 60 mph. The peak gust of 72 mph was measured between 0500-0530 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first major winter storm in quite a while brought an extended period of heavy snow to northern California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter 4SW Weed reported 16 inches of snow with 1.56 inches of water content in 15 hours ending at 06/0900 PST.   A spotter 2SSW Weed reported 10 inches of heavy wet snow in 12 hours ending at 06/0600 PST.  A spotter 1WSW Mount Shasta reported 8 inches of heavy wet snow in 12 hours ending at 06/0600 PST. A spotter at Mount Shasta City reported 6.5 inches of wet heavy snow in 10 hours ending at 06/0600 PST. A CoCoRahs observer 5.1 NW of Mount Shasta City reported a storm total of 16.1 inches in 24 hours ending around 06/0700 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold, upper-level closed low developed along the Central Coast and drifted south before stalling over southern California on the 23rd. Heavy showers began in the northern areas, spreading slowly south into San Diego County. The focus of the precipitation was over and west of the mountains on the 21st, as the low center just west of Oceanside drove southwesterly flow into the area. For the 22nd into the 23rd, the low shifted directly over San Diego, changing the flow to east/southeast and bringing the precipitation focus to the desert slopes and mountain ridges. Three-day storm total rainfall saw one quarter of an inch to one inch of rain for the coast and valley locations of Orange and San Diego Counties, while the Inland Empire saw up to 2.75 inches of rain on the slopes of the San Bernardino Mountains. Several roads were closed due to flooding in the Inland Empire, stalling a few vehicles and requiring swift water rescues. Heavy rain draining into a canyon near Moreno valley on the 21st resulted in flash flooding across a roadway, trapping vehicles and at least one person. The combination of one inch of rainfall in one hour and a storm pump failure resulted in flooding and a major rescue in the San Diego ���Midway��� area on the 21st as well. Very heavy rain near the Mexican Border on the 22nd caused several instances of flooding in the Tijuana River basin. A thunderstorm coming onshore near Solana Beach produced periods of dime to nickel-sized hail according to the lifeguards. The lower deserts were more varied in amounts ranging from one tenth of an inch to near three-quarters of an inch. The snow level started around 7000 feet on the 21st and dropped to 4000-5000 feet by the morning of the 22nd. Most locations above 4000 feet reported 1 to 3 inches of snow. However there were a few standout locations with heavy snow. Wrightwood, in the San Bernardino Mountains measured 11 to 13 inches at 6040 feet (spotter report). Pine Cove, in the Riverside County Mountains, measured 9 inches of snow at 6200 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Solana Beach lifeguards observed dime to nickel sized hail, along with periods of smaller hail, from a thunderstorm that moved onshore.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind blew down a tree which blocked all northbound lanes of Interstate 238 on the edge of San Leandro and fell on a car. Several cars then struck the downed tree before the highway was closed for one hour.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The San Bernardino Sun reported that firefighters from Colton, San Bernardino and Riverside were called to the Santa Ana River bed, near the intersection of Riverside Avenue and Key Street around 1900 PST, to rescue five homeless people trapped in the normally dry channel by rapidly rising flood waters. One man was trapped in a tree and had to be rescued by helicopter. The other four were rescued from a small island just down-river of the other man, once flood waters had receded. The man in the tree was treated at a hospital for minor hypothermia, but the others were uninjured.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold, upper-level closed low developed along the Central Coast and drifted south before stalling over southern California on the 23rd. Heavy showers began in the northern areas, spreading slowly south into San Diego County. The focus of the precipitation was over and west of the mountains on the 21st, as the low center just west of Oceanside drove southwesterly flow into the area. For the 22nd into the 23rd, the low shifted directly over San Diego, changing the flow to east/southeast and bringing the precipitation focus to the desert slopes and mountain ridges. Three-day storm total rainfall saw one quarter of an inch to one inch of rain for the coast and valley locations of Orange and San Diego Counties, while the Inland Empire saw up to 2.75 inches of rain on the slopes of the San Bernardino Mountains. Several roads were closed due to flooding in the Inland Empire, stalling a few vehicles and requiring swift water rescues. Heavy rain draining into a canyon near Moreno valley on the 21st resulted in flash flooding across a roadway, trapping vehicles and at least one person. The combination of one inch of rainfall in one hour and a storm pump failure resulted in flooding and a major rescue in the San Diego ���Midway��� area on the 21st as well. Very heavy rain near the Mexican Border on the 22nd caused several instances of flooding in the Tijuana River basin. A thunderstorm coming onshore near Solana Beach produced periods of dime to nickel-sized hail according to the lifeguards. The lower deserts were more varied in amounts ranging from one tenth of an inch to near three-quarters of an inch. The snow level started around 7000 feet on the 21st and dropped to 4000-5000 feet by the morning of the 22nd. Most locations above 4000 feet reported 1 to 3 inches of snow. However there were a few standout locations with heavy snow. Wrightwood, in the San Bernardino Mountains measured 11 to 13 inches at 6040 feet (spotter report). Pine Cove, in the Riverside County Mountains, measured 9 inches of snow at 6200 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter reported roadway flooding along east La Candena Drive, a frontage road of Interstate 215. A 500-600 foot swath of 1 foot-deep, standing water covered the roadway, stalling at least one vehicle.

A daily record for rainfall was set on this day at Lindbergh Field for a single day total of 1.46 inches of rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: There was one last round of north to northeast winds to end the year with 1036 mb surface high pressure rebuilding over Utah on the 29th, resulting in a 25 mb pressure gradient from Salt Lake City to San Diego. This event was not as widespread as those earlier in the month. The highest winds occurred where mountain waves surfaced below the Cajon pass and the Santa Ana Mountains and foothills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds began at the Pleasants Peak mesonet between 0500-0530 PST and ended between 1100-1130 PST on the 29th. During this time, winds were out of the northeast and east-northeast with gusts over 60 mph and several over 70 mph. The peak gust of 78 mph was measured twice between 0700-0900 PST.|High winds began at the Fremont Canyon RAWS between 0551-0651 PST and ended between 1051-1151 PST on the 29th. During this time, winds were out of the east-northeast sustained around 40 mph with a peak gust of 70 mph was measured twice between 0751-0851 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold trough of low pressure dropped down the West Coast, before closing off over southern California. This system brought light to locally moderate showers to most areas, except the Coachella Valley, on the afternoon and evening of the 6th. Light showers lingered into the morning of the 7th near the mountains. Rainfall totals for the coast and valleys were mostly light, generally ranging from one-tenth to one-third of an inch, with three-quarters of an inch to an inch for the lower mountains and foothills. The lower deserts generally received less than one-tenth of an inch, with the exception of Borrego Springs with 0.16 inches of rain. The snow level fell to between 3500 and 4000 feet, with 1 to 3 inches of snow below 6000 feet, and up to 6 inches above 6000 feet.On the 10th, a very cold trough of low pressure from the northwest moved through southern California, bringing significant cooling, low snow levels, gusty west winds and light to locally moderate precipitation along and west of the mountains. The snow level dropped to around 2500 feet, with accumulating snow of around one-half inch down to around 3000 feet in locations such as Hesperia and the Cajon Pass in San Bernardino County, and Julian in San Diego County. Up to 5 inches of snow fell above 4000 feet. Chains were required in most mountain areas as well. Gusty winds also accompanied the storm on the 10th, even resulting in some blowing dust in the Coachella Valley. Strong winds across the coastal waters, combined with a large swell and astronomical high tides, resulted in minor coastal flooding during the morning of the 11th in La Jolla, Imperial Beach and Newport Beach. Most flooding was limited to a few beach parking lots and some nearby surface streets.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds began at the Volcan Mountain SDGE mesonet between 0800 and 0810 PST on the 10th and ended between 1550 and 1600 PST the same day. During this time, winds gusted above 60 mph almost the entire time. This included a few gusts above 70 mph and the peak gust of 73 mph, which was measured between 1300 and 1310 PST. |High winds also occurred at the Harrison Park mesonet, between 1030 and 1430 PST on the 10th. During this time, sustained winds were around 45 mph with gusts over 60 mph. This included the peak gust of 63 mph, which was measured between 1340 and 1350 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system dropped heavy rain and a few inches of snow at higher levels. Behind the storm, the Sierra Nevada and Lake County received significant wind damage, in addition to 1 fatality and 2 injuries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong wind gusts of 50 to 80 mph were measured over South Lake County. Cow Mountain Ridge (3355ft) reported 78mph, whereas lower elevations reported 50-65mph. Damage reports consisted of several powerlines down, trees down onto cars, an RV park, and houses, thousands of power outages, and damage to two barns and fences. Largest damage areas were reported around Clearlake, Nice, and Upper Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system dropped heavy rain and a few inches of snow at higher levels. Behind the storm, the Sierra Nevada and Lake County received significant wind damage, in addition to 1 fatality and 2 injuries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong wind gusts of 70 to 90 mph were estimated or measured over far northern Sierra Nevada and southern Cascades region. A location 12 miles west of Caribou in Plumas County measured 86mph from the Mesonet, and a location 7 miles east-northeast of Mill Creek (6730ft) in Plumas County reported 87mph. Multiple trees were reported down, including multiple powerlines down and power outages. There was a report that strong winds blew a moving van off the road and into the Feather River, 1 mile northeast of Pulga in Butte County sometime during the event. The driver was injured and hospitalized with hypothermia, according to the Forest Service.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: NBC 7 reported waves flooding streets over Camino del Oro and Avenida de la Playa in La Jolla around 0730 PST, during the time of high tide. High tide that morning was just under 7 and a half feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system dropped heavy rain and a few inches of snow at higher levels. Behind the storm, the Sierra Nevada and Lake County received significant wind damage, in addition to 1 fatality and 2 injuries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong wind gusts of 60 to 90 mph were measured over the northern Sierra Nevada. A location 6 miles north-northwest of Downieville in Sierra County measured 88mph, and a location 11 miles south-southeast of Cisco (7100ft) in Placer County reported 90mph. Reports of several trees and limbs down, blocking roadways including Highways 49 and 50. There was a report of a tree falling on a powerline and starting a fire 4 miles northeast of Glencoe in Calaveras County. There was also a report of a rockslide 1 mile west-northwest of Strawberry in Tuolumne County that made a local road impassable. Local media reported 3 homes were damaged in Kyburz in El Dorado County. In addition, the high winds caused significant damage to the South Yuba Canal in the Bear Valley area east of Nevada City, cutting water flows from the mountains into the Grass Valley-Nevada City area, as reported by PG&E and Nevada Irrigation District (NID). The damage was widespread, with numerous large trees falling into and over the canal, breaking and collapsing flumes. The damage occurred in several sections of the 19-mile waterway which extends from lake Spaulding to the Scott Flat Lake area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 1042 mb high built in over Nevada on the 9th as the low moved off to the east with a 20-25 mb offshore pressure gradient setting up from Salt Lake City to San Diego. This produces gusty northeast winds and cool, dry air for southern California through the 10th. The San Diego County mountains and foothills were again the most windy areas for this event with gusts over 65 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds occurred at the Big Black Mountain mesonet between 1718-1937 PST on the 9th with sustained winds of 45-55 mph and a peak gust of 66 mph measured between 1927-1937 PST. A mesonet station 1 mile ESE of Alpine measured a peak gust of 65 mph around 2052 PST on the 9th as well.

CZ_NAME: SAN BERNARDINO AND RIVERSIDE COUNTY VALLEYS - THE INLAND EMPIRE
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 1042 mb high built in over Nevada on the 9th as the low moved off to the east with a 20-25 mb offshore pressure gradient setting up from Salt Lake City to San Diego. EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds occurred at the Highland Springs RAWS between 1116-1416 PST on the 9th. During this time, winds gusted over 59 mph with a peak gust of 60 mph measured between 1216-1316 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold snap across the area kept much of the valley overnight temperatures below freezing for the first week of December. This was followed by a low pressure system that brought additional cold weather, with very low elevation snow (300-500ft). The snow was significant due to its rarity at the lower elevations, as well as a temporary closures of I-5 in Shasta County and trans-Sierra I-80.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A cold storm system spread precipitation across northwest Shasta County for several hours. The low snow levels associated with this system resulted in snow to locations that do not normally receive accumulating snow. There were 3 inches of snow at Shasta Dam, 6-8 inches in the foothills including Sims, and up to 8-12 inches for the higher peak elevations. I-5 was closed for a period of time due to the wintery conditions and multiple car accidents.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold snap across the area kept much of the valley overnight temperatures below freezing for the first week of December. This was followed by a low pressure system that brought additional cold weather, with very low elevation snow (300-500ft). The snow was significant due to its rarity at the lower elevations, as well as a temporary closures of I-5 in Shasta County and trans-Sierra I-80.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The cold, low elevation snow system brought several inches of snow to the valley floor, which is rare for the area. Areas in Redding and Anderson (400-700ft elevations) received 4-6 inches of snow, with 1-2 inches farther south near Red Bluff and Cottonwood (300-500ft elevations). Locations near Dairyville also received 1-2 inches of snow.  Spotters reported non accumulating snow at elevations as low as 200ft.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold snap across the area kept much of the valley overnight temperatures below freezing for the first week of December. This was followed by a low pressure system that brought additional cold weather, with very low elevation snow (300-500ft). The snow was significant due to its rarity at the lower elevations, as well as a temporary closures of I-5 in Shasta County and trans-Sierra I-80.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The cold, low elevation snow system brought significant amounts to the motherlode foothills, with elevations as low as approximately 800 ft. Auburn (1000 ft elevation) received 1 inch of snow. Grass Valley and Nevada City area received anywhere from 4 to 8 inches, reported by spotters. Higher amounts were reported by spotters near Groveland (12) and Sonora (10). Placerville, Camino, and Somerset areas along and near Highway 50 received 5 to 8 inches. In addition to the snow, the very cold temperatures prompted Nevada and Tuolumne counties to open warming centers in Grass Valley and Sonora, respectively, for several days.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold snap across the area kept much of the valley overnight temperatures below freezing for the first week of December. This was followed by a low pressure system that brought additional cold weather, with very low elevation snow (300-500ft). The snow was significant due to its rarity at the lower elevations, as well as a temporary closures of I-5 in Shasta County and trans-Sierra I-80.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The cold, low elevation snow system dumped significant amounts of snow along the western slopes of the Sierra Nevada. Kirkwood ski resort reported 24-32 inches of snow along their slopes. Other ski resorts such as Sugarbowl and Sierra at Tahoe reported 18-22 inches. Near Donner Pass, areas received 18-22 inches of snow. Kingvale spotter reported 23 inches. Lower elevations such as Sonora (3500-4000ft elevations) reported 12-14 inches, Pacific House (3500ft) reported 11 inches, and Pollock Pines (3900ft) reported 6-8 inches. |In addition, due to multiple spinout accidents on I-80 during the event, Caltrans temporarily closed I-80 eastbound at Applegate (2000ft) and I-80 westbound at the stateline for approximately an hour (early morning on December 7th).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first major winter storm in quite a while brought an extended period of heavy snow to northern California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter in Tennant reported 9 inches of snow in 12 hours ending at 06/1200 PST.  The next day he reported a 48 hour total of 12 inches ending at 07/0800 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As high pressure over the Great Basin weakened, a shallow marine layer was allowed to reform along the coast, leading to dense fog conditions for many coastal locations. Visibility was reduced along portions of Interstates 8 and 15, as well as Highways 52 and 78 in San Diego County. Affected areas in Orange County included sections of Interstates 5 and 405, as well as Highways 73 and 55. Affected airports included Lindbergh Field, Brown Field, Montgomery Field, MCAS Miramar, Carlsbad, Imperial Beach Naval Auxiliary Landing Field and John Wayne Airport; all reported visibility of less than one-quarter of a mile at times overnight into the morning of the 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: John Wayne Airport ASOS reported visibilities of a quarter mile or less intermittently between 0000 and 0800 PST on the 9th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure brought high downslope winds in the lee of the Sierra Nevada.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The peak gust was measured 4 miles NW of Independence at 0542 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clearing skies and a cold air mass made for a frigid morning over northern California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The only reported low temperature in this zone was 26 degrees at Callahan.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoon moisture from the southeast, as well as the remnants of Tropical Storm Ivo, set off thunderstorms along and east of a line from Big Bear, south to Tecate, Mexico each afternoon/evening. These storms produced very heavy rain, with over 2 inches measured at many mountain and desert locations including Agua Caliente, Borrego Springs, Pine Hills and La Quinta. Borrego Springs and the southern Coachella Valley were the hardest hit areas for flooding on the 23rd and 25th, with several roads closed due to heavy water flow and washouts with mud and debris. Several cars were nearly submerged in flooded roadways in La Quinta and Indio, stranding drivers. The Indio gauge along the Whitewater Storm Channel experienced a sharp rise from near zero to 1080 cfs over approximately 30 minutes on the afternoon of the 25th. Highway 62 near Desert Hot Springs was also affected with very a very wide and deep area of flowing water, rocks and debris. Highway S22 near Borrego Springs suffered significant road damage and road closures for up to 24 hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter reported 1.24 inches of rain fell in 35 minutes near the intersection of Highway 111 and Indian Trail Road in Rancho Mirage, resulting in 1 foot of water in the roadway at the gutters. The Century Theatre at The River in Rancho Mirage also evacuated movie-goers due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong, upper-level low pressure system moved through southern California with gusty westerly winds along the mountain ridges and down the desert slopes. There were three periods of enhanced winds over the mountain ridges and through the aligned passes, with gusts 55-75 mph. Visibility was reduced in the Coachella Valley below the San Gorgonio Pass due to blowing sand and dust. No damage was reported, however. This event was also the first significant precipitation event of the winter season. The rainfall was likely a factor in the 318 accidents that occurred during the event, versus a normal day of 75 accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The observer at the Los Pinos fire lookout reported a peak wind gust of 66 mph, measured at 1308 PST on the 9th. The Volcan Mountain mesonet station reported wind gusts over 58 mph between 0200-0300 PST, with a peak gust of 64 mph recorded between 0230 and 0240 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: One man died of heat stroke in Death Valley National Park.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One man died of heat stroke in the southern end of Death Valley National Park.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported low temperatures ranged from 29 to 43 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system produced strong winds in the Owens Valley, resulting in dust storm conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dust storm conditions reduced visibility to one quarter mile along Highways 6 and 395 near and just north of Bishop.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific storm system brought high winds to the mountains and deserts of southeast California and southern Nevada.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees blew down onto power lines in Keeler. The peak gust was measured 4 miles NW of Independence at 2042 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific storm system brought high winds to the mountains and deserts of southeast California and southern Nevada.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds in Twentynine Palms tore half the shingles off the roof of one house and blew down two trees onto another house, causing minor damage.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds blew paper shingles off some homes and tore down a few small tree branches in Lenwood.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: South to southwest winds blew dense smoke from the massive Rim Fire near Yosemite  into the eastern Sierra of Alpine and El Dorado Counties between the 23rd and the 28th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dense smoke from the Rim Fire near Yosemite brought periodic low visibilities and poor air quality to areas from Alpine County north to South Lake Tahoe. Visibilities down to 1/4 mile were reported near Markleeville with visibility down to 1/2 mile at the South Lake Tahoe airport. The low visibility caused the cancellation of an air show at the airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lingering monsoon moisture from a strong push in late August continued to fuel thunderstorms over the Mojave Desert through early September. Several storms produced flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A vehicle was stuck in flood waters up to the windows at the intersection of Highway 95 and Goffs Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Ahead of a low pressure system, strong onshore winds caused for travel disruption for Northern California. The system then dropped over a foot of snow for the Sierra at higher elevations as it passed through the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The low pressure system brought the first significant snows of the winter season. The  snow was confined to the higher terrain  (mainly above 6500 ft), but did impact Interstate 80 and Donner Pass. Snow amounts reported by spotters include 9 at Kingvale, 14 at Soda Springs, 15 at Sugarbowl Peak, 12 at Sugarbowl base, 8.9 at Forni Ridge, 11.6 at 5 ENE of Kyburz, and 5.8 at 5 ENE Pinecrest.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lingering monsoon moisture from a strong push in late August continued to fuel thunderstorms over the Mojave Desert through early September. Several storms produced flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six to 12 inches of muddy water flowed across Highway 62 at Hendys Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large amount of subtropical moisture combined with good instability resulted in convective heavy rain showers and some localized flooding along the Sierra and Sierra foothills. First semi-widespread rain of the wet season.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Showers developed in a southwest to northeast line from southwest of Stockton area to northeast of Quincy. Trained spotters reported 1.50 of heavy rainfall as convective showers trained over the area all day long on Sept 2nd. Flooding was reported at 10:43 am PST along Highway 49 in Alta Sierra. Flooding was also reported at 10:46 am PST along Highways 49 and 20 in Nevada City. Rain continued through the early morning Sept. 3rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clover Fire began in the CAL FIRE Shasta-Trinity Unit in Shasta County, in the community of Igo, CA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Clover Fire developed in the community of Igo, 10 miles southwest of Redding, and forced many people to evacuate their homes. The fire grew to 8,073 acres, killed one 56-year old male who chose not to evacuate his home, and injured six people. The fire destroyed 68 residences and 128 outbuildings, and it damaged 5 additional residences and 10 outbuildings. Cost of the fire is unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lingering monsoon moisture continued to fuel thunderstorms over the Mojave Desert. A few storms produced flash flooding and severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three to six inches of water, mud, and debris covered Highway 62 between Utah Trail and Mesquite Springs Road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eight to 12 inches of water flowed across Paxton Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large area of surface high pressure built in over the Great Basin, creating approximately 14 millibars of offshore flow from Salt Lake City to the coast of San Diego. This led to temperatures in the low to mid 90s at the coast and strong gusty winds over the mountain ridges and through aligned canyons and passes. Riverside and Orange County were primarily affected on the 4th, then San Diego County on the 5th as the wind shifted more easterly. No wind damage was reported, but the strong winds led to the spread of the De Luz Fire on the Camp Pendleton marine base.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Sky Valley mesonet station reported winds gusts over 58 mph off and on from 0500-0700 PST on the 4th. During this time, a peak wind gust of 60 mph was measured twice between 0652 and 0702 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong, upper-level low pressure system moved through southern California with gusty westerly winds along the mountain ridges and down the desert slopes. There were three periods of enhanced winds over the mountain ridges and through the aligned passes, with gusts 55-75 mph. Visibility was reduced in the Coachella Valley below the San Gorgonio Pass due to blowing sand and dust. No damage was reported, however. This event was also the first significant precipitation event of the winter season. The rainfall was likely a factor in the 318 accidents that occurred during the event, versus a normal day of 75 accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Sky Valley mesonet station reported wind gusts over 58 mph off and on from 2000 PST on the 8th to 0400 PST on the 9th, with several gusts over 60 mph. During this time, a peak gust of 78 mph was recorded twice between 0222 and 0232 PST on the 9th. The Whitewater RAWS station reported a peak gust of 60 mph between 0150 and 0250 PST on the 9th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure off the central California coast contributed to the formation of thunderstorms in the Sierra on the 20th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny to nickel hail was reported in South Lake Tahoe with hail accumulations up to 2 inches deep.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first snowstorm of the season dropped snowfall over parts of Northwest California. Snow levels were low. This resulted in chain restrictions on the higher elevation roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were a few reports of snowfall meeting warning criteria, especially at lower elevations. Chain restrictions were required on some of the main roads and numerous traffic accidents were reported on these roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Ahead of a low pressure system, strong onshore winds caused for travel disruption for Northern California. The system then dropped over a foot of snow for the Sierra at higher elevations as it passed through the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong onshore winds brought down large trees for the Southern Sacramento Valley. Sacramento Executive AP peaked at 41mph, Sacramento International AP peaked at 46mph, and Vacaville/Nut Tree peaked at 36mph. Broadcast media reported several large trees down in Sacramento which hit houses, powerlines, and cars. A tree fell on a home near Sac State that caused significant roof damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated to scattered thunderstorms continued over the mountains, deserts, and portions of the Inland Empire, producing flash flooding each afternoon and evening through the 7th, with a brief lull in activity on the 1st. The hot spots on the 3rd were in the San Diego and Riverside County mountains, as well as the Coachella Valley. Storms in the Idyllwild/Pine Cove area produced flash flooding along Highway 243 and hail up to one-half inch. The Palm Springs area experienced flooding due to runoff from storms in the adjacent mountains, closing Araby Drive. Mountain storms also resulted in a flash flood and debris flow that raced down Tahquitz Creek and flowed through the Tahquitz Creek golf resort, closing Cathedral Canyon Drive at the Whitewater Wash. Areas in Riverside County also received accumulating small hail and severe winds from collapsing thunderstorms. Activity on the 4th and 5th favored similar areas, with more flash flooding. Runoff from storms over the Mountain Fire burn area in the San Jacinto Mountains resulted in a flash flood and debris flow 2 feet deep and 20 feet wide across Golf Club Drive in Cathedral City. Heavy rain also caused flooding on Highway 74 at McCall Road in Idyllwild, as well as Interstate 8 between Willows Road and Japatul. On the 6th, mud and water covered Highway S2 near the SR 79 intersection, closing that road due to multiple vehicles stuck in the mud. Pea to dime sized hail and damaging winds also accompanied these storms. Heavy rain caused minor road flooding in the Pine Valley area, with 1 to 2 inches of water flowing over several roadways. Highway 247 at Bessemer Mine Road had a 50-foot wide area of fast-moving water flowing across it, just east of Lucerne Valley. On the 7th, heavy rain near Forest Falls resulted in the normally dry Mill Creek running 2 feet deep and about 50 feet wide, stranding campers on one side of the creek. In San Diego County, there were 5 or 6 spots of water running across Highway 94 between Shockey Truck Trail and Sheridan Road with a maximum water depth of 6 inches. Waves of water up to 8 inches deep, with mud and debris, flowed rapidly over Highway 78 a few miles east of Julian. Highway 78 was impacted again as it dropped down into the desert, with 3 to 8 inches of water, in an area approximately 75 yards wide, flowing across the highway. Golf Club Drive in Cathedral City was closed again due to flooding from the Whitewater Wash.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The spotter was in Idyllwild traveling on Highway 243 to Pine Cove. There was 2 deep of 0.5 hail and 1-3 of water flowing across Highway243. Broken tree branches were all over the roadway from hail damage. A nearby COOP observer reported that the roads turned white, all covered with accumulating hail.

Storms in the Idyllwild/Pine Cove area produced flash flooding along Highway 243 and hail up to one-half inch. The Palm Springs area experienced flooding due to runoff from storms in the adjacent mountains, closing Araby Drive. Mountain storms also resulted in a flash flood and debris flow that raced down Tahquitz Creek and flowed through the Tahquitz Creek golf resort, closing Cathedral Canyon Drive at the Whitewater Wash. Areas in Riverside County also received accumulating small hail and severe winds from collapsing thunderstorms. Activity on the 4th and 5th favored similar areas, with more flash flooding. Runoff from storms over the Mountain Fire burn area in the San Jacinto Mountains resulted in a flash flood and debris flow 2 feet deep and 20 feet wide across Golf Club Drive in Cathedral City. Heavy rain also caused flooding on Highway 74 at McCall Road in Idyllwild, as well as Interstate 8 between Willows Road and Japatul. On the 6th, mud and water covered Highway S2 near the SR 79 intersection, closing that road due to multiple vehicles stuck in the mud. Pea to dime sized hail and damaging winds also accompanied these storms. Heavy rain caused minor road flooding in the Pine Valley area, with 1 to 2 inches of water flowing over several roadways. Highway 247 at Bessemer Mine Road had a 50-foot wide area of fast-moving water flowing across it, just east of Lucerne Valley. On the 7th, heavy rain near Forest Falls resulted in the normally dry Mill Creek running 2 feet deep and about 50 feet wide, stranding campers on one side of the creek. In San Diego County, there were 5 or 6 spots of water running across Highway 94 between Shockey Truck Trail and Sheridan Road with a maximum water depth of 6 inches. Waves of water up to 8 inches deep, with mud and debris, flowed rapidly over Highway 78 a few miles east of Julian. Highway 78 was impacted again as it dropped down into the desert, with 3 to 8 inches of water, in an area approximately 75 yards wide, flowing across the highway. Golf Club Drive in Cathedral City was closed again due to flooding from the Whitewater Wash.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm spotter reported flooding of roads in Pine Valley with two homeowners shoveling to divert waters from their homes. Further south in Lake Morena, a resident reported minor road flooding at Buckman Springs Road and Lake Morena Dr. His rain gauge measured 0.81 inches of rainfall in 1 hour. The nearby Campo COOP observer reported 0.94 inches of rain in 45 minutes during this time.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant heavy rain on the 3rd through 5th resulted in devastating flooding and debris flows in the canyon of the Riverside Mountains where the Zen Mountain Center is located. The Center sustained heavy damage from debris flows on the 4th and 5th and it is unclear which particular day was worse. On the 4th, approximately 3 to 5 feet of mud and rocks flowed through the retreat, half-burying cars and cabins. On the 5th, the second flow brought several more feet of mud and debris, fully burying a few cars. The flows broke plumbing pipes and uncovered buried phone lines. The various buildings sustained significant damage as well, with several feet of mud inside and water damage. Much of the walkways and common areas were unrecognizable due to the washouts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: North wind event in the Central Valley caused thousands of power outages. Peak wind gusts ranged from 35 to 50 mph across the valley and delta.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: North wind event produced sustained winds of 15 to 25mph with gusts up to 35 to 45mph for the Southern Sacramento Valley. The power went out for nearly 11,000 customers in Rio Linda and North Highland (northern Sacramento) areas. Additional several hundred power outages were recorded in West Sacramento, Orangevale, and Fair Oaks. Total power outages in the Sacramento metro were estimated to be 16,000 customers due to strong winds. Two of the outages were said to be caused by falling tree limbs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Ahead of a low pressure system, strong onshore winds caused for travel disruption for Northern California. The system then dropped over a foot of snow for the Sierra at higher elevations as it passed through the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Onshore winds increased in the afternoon Oct. 27th ahead of the strong trough. Winds in Tracy measured 44mph, Stockton measured 44mph, and Campo Seco measured 49mph. Blowing dust greatly restricted visibility and brought traffic to a slow crawl on highly traveled roads (I-5 and I-580) in the late afternoon and evening hours. The California Highway Patrol reported that six big rigs on I-580 were knocked over between 6pm PDT Sunday to 5am PDT Monday. Two drivers suffered minor to moderate injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another surge of monsoon moisture fueled widespread thunderstorms over the Mojave Desert and southern Great Basin. Many storms produced flash flooding, and isolated severe weather also occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water and debris flowed over Highway 190 between mile markers 121 and 128.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant surge of subtropical moisture arrived on the 29th ahead of a weather system coming up from Mexico. Combined with monsoonal moisture from the southeast, this resulted in scattered thunderstorms over the mountains, deserts, and portions of the Inland Empire each afternoon and evening through the 31st. Thunderstorms drifted into the Inland Empire from the adjacent mountains due to the easterly steering flow, as well as along the Elsinore Convergence Zone, in western Riverside County, on the 29th and 30th. The main area of flooding on the 29th occurred in the Riverside area, submerging vehicles and toppling large trees, due to rain-soaked ground and winds. The underpass along 14th Street west of Highway 91 experienced so much rain that it was still flooded the following morning. Though the Riverside Airport did not receive much in the way of rainfall; the gauge at UC Riverside received 1.2 inches of rain. The Inland Empire bore the brunt of the rainfall again on the 30th, adding to the existing flooding issues and creating new ones near Banning and Perris with flooded roadways and an RV park. The flooding in Banning was a debris flow from the Silver Fire burn scar. The only major flooding on the 31st was confined to the high desert along Highway 247 between Reche Road and Lynn Street.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported that the 60 freeway was flooded, with 4 or 5 vehicles stuck in flood waters under the Market Street overpass. One motorist reported that the flood waters were moving his vehicle. Lanes 2, 3, and 4 of Highway 91 were flooded, with vehicles flooded out and sliding, including a school bus. Riverside City College cancelled classes due to flooding at the Landis Performing Arts Center and the campus quadrangle. Several other cars were flooded in parking lots near 14th Street, as well as basement flooding at a business there.

CZ_NAME: SANTA LUCIA MOUNTAINS AND LOS PADRES NATIONAL FOREST
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: August represented another dry month across the area. Fuel moisture continued to run near all time record low values and this contributed to rapid fire growth for several small fires across the area. The Mission Wildfire, on the grounds of Fort Hunter Liggett, was the most substantial fire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mission Wildfire broke out on Fort Hunter Liggett on August 22 and quickly grew to consume 4500 acres. One private home was threatened but not destroyed. The fire was contained on August 26.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoon moisture from the southeast, as well as the remnants of Tropical Storm Ivo, set off thunderstorms along and east of a line from Big Bear, south to Tecate, Mexico each afternoon/evening. These storms produced very heavy rain, with over 2 inches measured at many mountain and desert locations including Agua Caliente, Borrego Springs, Pine Hills and La Quinta. Borrego Springs and the southern Coachella Valley were the hardest hit areas for flooding on the 23rd and 25th, with several roads closed due to heavy water flow and washouts with mud and debris. Several cars were nearly submerged in flooded roadways in La Quinta and Indio, stranding drivers. The Indio gauge along the Whitewater Storm Channel experienced a sharp rise from near zero to 1080 cfs over approximately 30 minutes on the afternoon of the 25th. Highway 62 near Desert Hot Springs was also affected with very a very wide and deep area of flowing water, rocks and debris. Highway S22 near Borrego Springs suffered significant road damage and road closures for up to 24 hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The USGS stream gage at Palm Canyon Creek reported a 2.7 ft. rise between 1500 and 1600 PST, with an estimated flow of 363 cfs.

CZ_NAME: IMPERIAL COUNTY EXCEPT THE LOWER COLORADO RIVER VALLEY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed across portions of far southwest Arizona and southeast California during the afternoon hours on August 22nd, and they produced strong and gusty outflow winds in excess of 40 mph. The winds were sufficient to generate dense blowing sand and dust, lowering visibilities to below one quarter mile across portions of Imperial county. As a result, dust storm conditions developed which led to the issuance of both Blowing Dust Advisories and Dust Storm Warnings for portions of Imperial county. No accidents or injuries were reported as a result of the dense blowing sand and dust.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed across portions of far southwest Arizona and southeast California during the afternoon hours on August 22nd, and they generated strong gusty outflow winds in excess of 50 mph. The gusty winds produced dense blowing sand and dust across portions of Imperial county, affecting communities such as Imperial and Holtville, as well as motorists on area roads such as Interstate 8. At 1602PST a Blowing Dust Advisory was issued for Imperial county and it was upgraded to a Dust Storm Warning at 1744PST. The warning remained in effect through 1900PST. According to a trained spotter in Holtville, at 1738PST winds gusts estimated at around 50 mph produced a dust storm. Dense blowing dust and sand lowered visibility to near zero miles. At 1755PST, the official Imperial ASOS weather station reported one quarter mile visibility in blowing dust, along with a wind gust measured at 44 mph. No injuries or accidents were reported due to the dust storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moisture from the south and instability from the heatwave brought thunderstorms up along the Sierra Nevada slopes and northern mountains, Monday through Thursday afternoons. Some lower elevations saw showers on Wednesday as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed in the afternoon along the Sierra Nevada crest and spread eastward. Witnesses reported a 66-year old female died in a campground after a lightning strike hit the area. No enter/exit woulds reported to confirm a direct hit.  However, evidence would suggest cardiac arrest due to a lightning strike was likely the cause of death.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Copious amounts of tropical moisture streamed northward into far southeastern California on August 24th, ahead of dissipating former tropical storm Ivo. The atmosphere over the lower California deserts became very moist and unstable, and scattered strong thunderstorms developed during the early afternoon hours. The storms generated copious amounts of rain, with rain rates in excess of 3 inches per hour. Flash flooding developed across portions of Imperial and eastern Riverside counties, especially in the area to the west of the Salton Sea. Some of the storms  produced gusty and damaging winds in excess of 60 mph, moderate hail and dense blowing dust. Numerous Flash Flood Warnings, Small Stream Flood Advisories, Severe Thunderstorm Warnings and Significant Weather Advisories were issued mainly during the afternoon hours for the deserts of southeast California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Copious amounts of tropical moisture from dissipating former tropical storm Ivo spread north into Imperial and eastern Riverside counties on August 24th and this led to a very moist and unstable airmass. Scattered strong thunderstorms with intense rainfall developed across portions of northwest Imperial and south central Riverside counties during the early afternoon hours. Rainfall rates based on radar were in excess of 3 inches per hour at times. The heavy rains led to significant flash flooding to the west of the Salton Sea, affecting areas from Salton City southeastward along highway 86 towards Westmoreland. A Flash Flood Warning was initially issued at 1207PST and it continued in effect until about 1700PST. Radar indicated that some areas west of the Salton Sea and along highway 86 received more than 6 inches of rainfall during the afternoon. According to California state officials, significant flash flooding occurred along highway 86; at 1442PST the California Highway Patrol indicated that Highways 78 and 86 were impassable about 9 miles southeast of Winona. Vehicles were stranded in water near the intersection of Highways 78 and 86. At 1320PST, law enforcement also reported flash flooding in Salton City; cars were stuck in water and the area washes were running heavily. Thunderstorms rains had dissipated by 1700PST and the Flash Flood Warning was replaced by an areal Flood Warning which remained in effect through 2100PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Rim Fire was a wildfire that began in the Groveland Range District of the Stanislaus National Forest on August 17th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Rim Fire was a wildfire that started on August 17th at around 3:15pm PDT in Tuolumne County. It was located in the Groveland Range District of Stanislaus National Forest. The fire grew to be the 3rd largest wildfire in California's history, at 257,314 acres. For the TOTAL FIRE EVENT: It cost approximately $127.2 million, caused 10 injuries, burned 11 residences, 3 commercial properties, and 98 outbuildings. For the month of SEPTEMBER ONLY: 34,358 more acres, additional $65.8 million, 5 additional injuries, 0 new residences, 0 new commercial properties, and 1 new outbuildings.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: From the 26rd through the end of the month, smoke from the massive Rim Fire near Yosemite brought periods of low visibility and unhealthy air quality to portions of eastern California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dense smoke and the associated poor air quality from the Rim Fire continued to periodically affect Alpine County through the end of the month although areas north near South Lake Tahoe generally saw some improvement after the 28th. Medical facilities in the South Lake Tahoe area noted around 5 to 10 visits per day related to respiratory problems (versus 1 or 2 a day normally in late summer) through the 28th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Silver Fire started due to lightning around 1305 PST on the 7th, along Poppet Flats Road near Hwy 243, south of Banning in Riverside County, with a critical rate of spread. Strong Banning pass breezes forced extreme growth according to fire personnel and smoke reduced visibility into Palm Springs on the first day. Mandatory evacuations for the communities of Poppet Flats, Twin Pines and Silent Valley. Evacuation warnings/voluntary were also issued for Diamond Zen Center, Pine Cove & Camp Azalea. Residents were instructed to evacuate through Hwy 243, south through Hwy 74 in Idyllwild to Hemet. Forty-seven people from the Twin Pines Ranch (Boys Youth Camp) were escorted down the mountain. The fire eventually split into two directions, moving into Poppet Flats, Silent Valley Club, Twin Pines and Cabazon, prompting mandatory evacuations to be expanded to the lower Cabazon area-Peach Street, Plum Street, Eucalyptus Street, and Riza Avenue, Ida Avenue, Helen Avenue East to Elm Street. Repeater sites, containing equipment for Department of Transportation, Department of Parks and Recreation, Cal Fire, CHP and Riverside County Fire, at Mt. Edna and Vista Grande were in the path of the fire. Black Mountain was evacuated and closed as a precaution. Boulder Basin and Black Mountain campgrounds on Black Mountain road were closed through august 15th.  Highway 243 was closed from 10.3 miles west to 1.5 miles west of Banning. 537 residences were threatened, 2 damaged and 26 destroyed, along with one commercial building and 21 outbuildings. Injuries to 12 firefighters and one civilian were reported. The fire burned 20,292 acres before being fully contained around 1840 PST on the 12th. This fire also had high media coverage, including national.

CZ_NAME: NORTHEAST SISKIYOU AND NORTHWEST MODOC COUNTIES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clearing skies and a cold air mass made for cold morning temperatures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported low temperatures ranged from 25 to 29 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant surge of subtropical moisture arrived on the 29th ahead of a weather system coming up from Mexico. Combined with monsoonal moisture from the southeast, this resulted in scattered thunderstorms over the mountains, deserts, and portions of the Inland Empire each afternoon and evening through the 31st. Thunderstorms drifted into the Inland Empire from the adjacent mountains due to the easterly steering flow, as well as along the Elsinore Convergence Zone, in western Riverside County, on the 29th and 30th. The main area of flooding on the 29th occurred in the Riverside area, submerging vehicles and toppling large trees, due to rain-soaked ground and winds. The underpass along 14th Street west of Highway 91 experienced so much rain that it was still flooded the following morning. Though the Riverside Airport did not receive much in the way of rainfall; the gauge at UC Riverside received 1.2 inches of rain. The Inland Empire bore the brunt of the rainfall again on the 30th, adding to the existing flooding issues and creating new ones near Banning and Perris with flooded roadways and an RV park. The flooding in Banning was a debris flow from the Silver Fire burn scar. The only major flooding on the 31st was confined to the high desert along Highway 247 between Reche Road and Lynn Street.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fire rescue officials reported that portions of Valley of the Falls Drive were closed due to water flowing over the road from Clarks Creek and Prospect Creek. Less than an hour later, fire officials reported that Prospect and Clarks Creeks were out of their banks. The intersection of Valley of the Falls Drive and Canyon Lane was also closed to all vehicles due to a mud slide.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong push of monsoon moisture fueled thunderstorms over the Mojave Desert and southern Great Basin for several days, peaking on the 25th. Many storms produced flash flooding, and isolated severe weather also occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Interstate 40 was closed at Highway 95, as high water and thick mud stranded several vehicles. A 500 pound propane tank was washed off its foundation. Several stretches of BNSF track totaling about 2.6 miles in length were damaged, and a bridge in the Sacramento Wash was undermined, suspending freight line and Amtrak service.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong push of monsoon moisture fueled thunderstorms over the Mojave Desert and southern Great Basin for several days, peaking on the 25th. Many storms produced flash flooding, and isolated severe weather also occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mud covered Essex Road, with several cars stuck in the mud.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surge of monsoonal moisture brought another round of strong thunderstorms and flash flooding to the Antelope Valley. Heavy rain and flash flooding was reported across Leona Valley and near Quartz Hill High School.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters reported widespread flash flooding across the communities of Quartz Hill and Leona Valley. The area around 60th Street and Quartz Hill High School experienced flash flooding as well as the area around 90th Street West and Elizabeth Lake Road in Leona Valley.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water and debris washed over Highway 62 from seven miles east of Twentynine Palms to Shelton Road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water and mud flowed across Highway 62.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Amboy Road was flooded and impassable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong push of monsoon moisture fueled thunderstorms over the Mojave Desert and southern Great Basin for several days, peaking on the 25th. Many storms produced flash flooding, and isolated severe weather also occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway 127 washed out just north of Powerline Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hot temperatures and plenty of moisture combined to bring strong thunderstorms with hail and heavy rain on the 2nd and 3rd. A slow-moving thunderstorm with very heavy rain was responsible for a large debris flow onto Interstate 80 which closed the freeway on the 2nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eight homes along highway 182 in Bridgeport received water damage including one home with a flooded basement and another with several inches of water on the main floor. Time estimated based on period of heavy rain in radar.

Another spotter reported water over another spot along Highway 18, estimated to be 50ft wide and more than 2 feet deep, with flow carrying boulders across the road. Up the mountain west of these locations, a spotter recorded a little over one inch of rain in 20 minutes and approximately 3 inches of water over some roadways.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent cutoff upper-level low slowly drifted to the south along the central California coast drawing subtropical moisture. This set off thunderstorms over the San Bernardino County mountains and deserts. The majority of the activity occurred in the high desert, including significant rainfall west of Victorville, where radar estimated up to 7 inches of rain at some locations, damaging roads and filling the normally dry Lake El Mirage. A few storms produced flash flooding east of Interstate 15, in the form of a foot of water flowing across Locust Street at State Route 247. Minor flooding also occurred near Apple Valley along Highway 18 at Milpas Road and Highway 247 at Post Office Road. A strong storm on the 18th also produced a land-spout tornado on the west side of the storm near Helendale.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter called to report street flooding near the intersection of Old Woman Springs Road (Highway 247) and Locust Street. Locust Street was impassable with 1 foot of water estimated to be flowing over the road.  California Highway Patrol reported flooding with debris on the road along highway 247 at Midway Avenue.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An employee at UC Riverside estimated wind gusts up to 70 mph with very heavy rain and reduced visibility. Nearby, a large tree fell on the Rubidoux Boulevard off-ramp along Highway 60. Several other locations reported branches down, including a large tree that was uprooted and fell on a house along Chapman Street west of Magnolia Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another surge of monsoon moisture fueled widespread thunderstorms over the Mojave Desert and southern Great Basin. Many storms produced flash flooding, and isolated severe weather also occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water and mud were rushing down the street at Interstate 40 and Nebo Street. Four power poles were knocked down by flash flooding near National Trails Highway and Minneola Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed across portions of Imperial county during the evening hours on September 9th. Due to the moist and unstable nature of the atmosphere, the storms generated strong, gusty winds as well as large hail. Some of the thunderstorms affected the community of Brawley, and Severe Thunderstorm Warnings were issued as a result of the strong convection.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Isolated evening thunderstorms developed across portions of Imperial county on September 9th, and they affected the community of Brawley. Due to the moist and and unstable nature of the atmosphere, some of the storms were able to generate strong and gusty outflow winds. A trained weather spotter in Brawley estimated a peak wind gust to 60 mph. According to an article in the Imperial Valley Press, a large tree fell over on top of a house in the 1200 block of E street in Brawley due to the strong thunderstorm winds. The article mentioned that a number of trees in the area were downed by the strong and gusty winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system approached California and brought thunderstorms along the higher terrain Sunday through Thursday, Aug. 18-22. Strongest thunderstorm development occurred Wednesday.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms developed along the Sierra and southern Cascades. One thunderstorm, in particular, strengthened over Lassen National Park. As this storm slowly moved north over the area, it dropped a lot of 1 inch hail and heavy rain. This was reported by a park ranger located at the park entrance near Manzanita Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A push of monsoon moisture fueled scattered thunderstorms over the Mojave Desert. Some storms produced flash flooding and severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew down trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level Pacific low pressure system brought strong, gusty and damaging winds to portions of southeast California on April 8th, especially the lower deserts of Imperial county. Gusty southwest to west winds developed during the morning hours, and continued into the early evening, prompting issuance of numerous products, including Wind Advisories, Blowing Dust Advisories, and High Wind Warnings. Peak gusts in excess of 60 mph were seen at times, strong enough to damage area trees and weaker structures such as sheds. The strong gradient winds were not associated with thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A powerful spring storm brought windy conditions to much of eastern Riverside county during the day on April 8th. Wind gusts in excess of 40 mph were common, and led to the issuance of a Wind Advisory which ran from 10 am PDT to 8 pm PDT. According to an on-line article posted on the KESQ.COM website, at 205 pm a dust devil struck the Salton City RV Resort in Salton City California. The dust devil combined with the already strong and gusty winds to produce damage at the RV park. Awnings were down, some siding was ripped off and a water pipe was broken. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The high pressure ridge over the region began to rebuild once again by July 17th. The flow aloft remained southwesterly, and skies remained generally clear until the 18th. Some development of convective clouds (i.e., mainly in the form of cumulus clouds) returned to the higher elevations of the southern Sierra Nevada by July 19th, then began to increase the 20th as the flow aloft turned more southerly and brought more moisture over the central California interior. By the 21st, the mountains and desert were once again under the influence of southeasterly monsoonal flow, and showers and thunderstorms returned.  One thunderstorm that moved through Grant Grove knocked down a few trees and power lines.||Even more active weather occurred in the Kern and Tulare County mountains, as well as the Kern County desert on July 22nd. During this time, monsoonal moisture was abundant with increased atmospheric instability which led to the issuance of some flash flood warnings in the Kern and Tulare County mountains and even a flash flood and severe thunderstorm warning in the Kern County desert. Flash flooding due to slow moving thunderstorms occurred at several locations, including Pine Mountain Club, Johnsondale (in the Tulare County mountains), and in the Kern County desert south of Ridgecrest, along Red Rock-Randsburg Road, a favored locale for flash flooding due to the topography in this area. This desert road was shut down during the late afternoon and evening hours due to both flooding and debris flow. Garlock Road near Randsburg also flooded, just west of the junction with U.S. 395, and U.S. 395 was closed north of Johannesburg due to flooding. Around one inch to one and a half inches of rain fell in a relatively short period of time in these areas, with Pine Mountain Club getting 1.86 inch of rain in less than an hour, and hail up to a half inch in diameter fell near Pine Mountain Club. In addition, some thunderstorms drifted from the southern Sierra Nevada into the foothills, and eventually the San Joaquin Valley. Both Fresno and Lemoore reported thunderstorms at their airports, and a thunderstorm passed over the National Weather Service office at Hanford during the afternoon. ||Monsoonal moisture tracked northward on the 23rd, and Merced reported some light rain as showers passed over the area. Thunderstorms initiated over southeastern Tulare County, and parts of the Kern County mountains, including the Piute Mountains and Walker Pass during the afternoon. A flash flood warning was even issued for the Piute Mountains by late in the afternoon as a strong thunderstorm developed over this area and produced heavy rain.||The monsoonal moisture continued to wrap around the upper-level level ridge over the Desert Southwest. The flow around the ridge brought repeated surges of this warm moist air, with mountain and desert thunderstorms continuing through July 28th. Debris clouds from mountain thunderstorms moved over parts of the southern San Joaquin Valley on the 26th, bringing light rain. Bakersfield recorded a trace of rain, but this was the first rain to fall in that city since records began in 1889,||Thunderstorms brought heavy rain to parts of the Indian Wells Valley during the evening of July 27th. Small hail fell on southwest Ridgecrest. The next day, outflow winds from a collapsing thunderstorm caused a gust to 46 mph on part of the China Lake Naval Weapons Test Center range. Another thunderstorm brought heavy rain to the southern Tulare County mountains. The Mahogany RAWS in the southern Tulare County mountains received 2.24 inch  of rain from the storm.||Fresno ended July with an average temperature of 87.1 degrees, for its 3rd warmest July on record. Bakersfield was slightly warmer with an average temperature of 87.6 degrees, tying its 6th warmest July since records began.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sherman Pass Road was closed due to flash flooding and mud slides. Severe cars were stranded and rescued by authorities.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A push of monsoonal moisture produced thunderstorms across the Antelope Valley. Some of these thunderstorms were severe, producing damaging wind gusts which downed power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported downed power lines along 4th Street East and East Avenue L4 due to thunderstorm winds. Wind gusts were estimated to be around 60 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms developed across portions of eastern Riverside county during the afternoon hours on September 8th. Due to the rather moist and unstable nature of the atmosphere, the storms were able to generate gusty outflow winds strong enough to blow down power poles. Most of the thunderstorms formed over open country, however a few were able to affect communities such as Blythe. Severe Thunderstorm Warnings were issued as a result of the strong convection.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms developed across far eastern portions of Riverside county during the afternoon hours on September 8th. The thunderstorms generated strong, gusty and eventually damaging outflow winds estimated to be at least 60 mph in strength. One of the stronger storms affected the community of Blythe. Local law enforcement reported that power poles were blown down near the intersection of State Route 78 and 16th Avenue, about 4 miles east southeast of the Blythe airport. A Severe Thunderstorm Warning was issued for the Blythe area starting at 1335PST  and continuing until 1430PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another surge of monsoon moisture fueled widespread thunderstorms over the Mojave Desert and southern Great Basin. Many storms produced flash flooding, and isolated severe weather also occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway 62 was flooded at Mesquite Springs Road. 2.48 inches of rain fell in one hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated to scattered thunderstorms continued over the mountains, deserts, and portions of the Inland Empire, producing flash flooding each afternoon and evening through the 7th, with a brief lull in activity on the 1st. The hot spots on the 3rd were in the San Diego and Riverside County mountains, as well as the Coachella Valley. Storms in the Idyllwild/Pine Cove area produced flash flooding along Highway 243 and hail up to one-half inch. The Palm Springs area experienced flooding due to runoff from storms in the adjacent mountains, closing Araby Drive. Mountain storms also resulted in a flash flood and debris flow that raced down Tahquitz Creek and flowed through the Tahquitz Creek golf resort, closing Cathedral Canyon Drive at the Whitewater Wash. Areas in Riverside County also received accumulating small hail and severe winds from collapsing thunderstorms. Activity on the 4th and 5th favored similar areas, with more flash flooding. Runoff from storms over the Mountain Fire burn area in the San Jacinto Mountains resulted in a flash flood and debris flow 2 feet deep and 20 feet wide across Golf Club Drive in Cathedral City. Heavy rain also caused flooding on Highway 74 at McCall Road in Idyllwild, as well as Interstate 8 between Willows Road and Japatul. On the 6th, mud and water covered Highway S2 near the SR 79 intersection, closing that road due to multiple vehicles stuck in the mud. Pea to dime sized hail and damaging winds also accompanied these storms. Heavy rain caused minor road flooding in the Pine Valley area, with 1 to 2 inches of water flowing over several roadways. Highway 247 at Bessemer Mine Road had a 50-foot wide area of fast-moving water flowing across it, just east of Lucerne Valley. On the 7th, heavy rain near Forest Falls resulted in the normally dry Mill Creek running 2 feet deep and about 50 feet wide, stranding campers on one side of the creek. In San Diego County, there were 5 or 6 spots of water running across Highway 94 between Shockey Truck Trail and Sheridan Road with a maximum water depth of 6 inches. Waves of water up to 8 inches deep, with mud and debris, flowed rapidly over Highway 78 a few miles east of Julian. Highway 78 was impacted again as it dropped down into the desert, with 3 to 8 inches of water, in an area approximately 75 yards wide, flowing across the highway. Golf Club Drive in Cathedral City was closed again due to flooding from the Whitewater Wash.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Sky Valley mesonet station recorded sustained winds greater than 40 mph from 1030 to 1200 PST. During this time, two gusts of 61 mph were measured between 1030 and 1040 PST. Two gusts of 58 mph were also recorded between 1050 and 1100 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Copious moisture was already in place when a cutoff low moved through southwest Oregon and northern California...providing an upper level trigger for thunderstorm development.  Many thunderstorms developed, with over 10000 lightning strikes recorded.  Some of the thunderstorms became severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public reported large hail;  the largest hail was at least 1.0 inches in diameter.   Holes were punched in plastic greenhouses and there was some agricultural and other vegetation damage.  Hail duration was 10 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lingering monsoon moisture continued to fuel thunderstorms over the Mojave Desert. A few storms produced flash flooding and severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water, mud, and debris crossed Highway 190 between Zabriskie Point and the Furnace Creek Inn, as well as between Saline Valley Road and Panamint Springs. One mile of the road was washed out, and several vehicles were stuck. Mud also washed across Scotty's Castle Road.
